terms.definition: The units and records actually included in a particular analysis, which may differ from those initially eligible or enrolled.
Distortion caused when post-entry loss, withdrawal, missing outcomes, or exclusion selects an analysis set in a way related to comparison group and outcome or its predictors.
aliases: bias due to loss to follow-up

terms.definition: A systematic distortion of a study estimate or conclusion introduced through design, conduct, measurement, analysis, or reporting.
Misclassification whose error mechanism differs according to the status of another variable in the analysis, such as outcome classification differing by exposure group.
Systematic distortion arising from how an exposure, outcome, covariate, or other study variable is measured, classified, recalled, recorded, or collected.
Keeping a specified person or role unaware of specified study information, such as group assignment, to block that knowledge from influencing conduct or assessment.
Assignment of a unit to an incorrect category for an exposure, outcome, or other variable.
An information-bias mechanism in which an assessor's knowledge or predisposition influences a judgment, measurement, classification, or recording decision.
Distortion of the accessible evidence base when a whole study's chance of becoming available depends on the nature or strength of its results.
Unpredictable variation that changes across repetitions and contributes imprecision rather than a stable systematic shift.
Distortion caused when a rule governing inclusion in a sample or analysis changes the target frequency, association, or comparison.
Result-dependent choice or completeness of outcomes, time points, or analyses reported from within a study that measured or planned more.
A selection mechanism in which only cases that remained alive, functioning, successful, persistent, or otherwise observable pass into the analyzed set.
The population or complete set of cases about which a stated estimate or conclusion is intended to speak.
The degree to which a result can support an inference in a population or setting different from the one in which the study comparison was estimated.
The extent to which a measurement, estimate, or inference supports the intended interpretation for its stated target and conditions.
